peptides.name: BPC-157
peptides.shortSummary: Frequently discussed in relation to tissue recovery and gut comfort.
peptides.overview: BPC-157 is a synthetic peptide that appears in research discussions about soft-tissue recovery and digestive comfort. It is studied largely in preclinical settings.
peptides.mechanism: This peptide is frequently discussed in relation to angiogenesis (new blood vessel formation) and connective-tissue signaling in research literature.

news.summary: Regulatory attention centers on the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting on July 23-24, 2026, now about eight days away. The committee will weigh seven nominated research peptides - BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, MOTS-c, Emideltide (DSIP), Semax, and Epitalon - against the four statutory 503A factors (characterization, safety, effectiveness, and historical use); the FDA's own briefing materials recommend against adding any of them, and any committee vote is advisory and would still require FDA rulemaking. On the research side, new analyses this week link tirzepatide to lower major adverse cardiovascular events and mortality, and report no excess diabetic retinopathy versus matched controls over a year in type 1 diabetes. This is not medical advice.

items: Semaglutide — Ozempic (2017), Wegovy (2021), Rybelsus (2019); type 2 diabetes & obesity.
Tirzepatide — Mounjaro (2022), Zepbound (2023); type 2 diabetes & obesity.
Liraglutide — Victoza (2010), Saxenda (2014); type 2 diabetes & obesity.
PT-141 / Bremelanotide — Vyleesi (2019); HSDD in premenopausal women.
Sermorelin — formerly FDA-approved (Geref, discontinued as a branded product); now supplied mainly via compounding pharmacies.
Elamipretide / SS-31 — Forzinity (FDA accelerated approval, Sep 2025); Barth syndrome — the first mitochondria-targeted therapy. Confirmatory Phase 3b/4 (4TAZPower) ongoing.
Retatrutide — GLP-1/GIP/glucagon triple agonist; Phase 3; NDA anticipated (not yet approved).
Cagrilintide / CagriSema — amylin analog (± semaglutide); Phase 3; under regulatory development.
Amycretin — GLP-1/amylin co-agonist (oral & injectable); Phase 3 initiated 2026.

Compounding reclassification (a compounding decision, not a drug approval): BPC-157, TB-500, MOTS-c, KPV, Semax, Epitalon, Emideltide (DSIP) — under FDA PCAC review, meeting July 23–24, 2026, for the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List. FDA's July 2026 briefing materials recommend NOT adding any of the seven; committee vote and any rulemaking still follow.
Approved abroad but NOT by the US FDA: Thymosin Alpha-1 (thymalfasin/Zadaxin — many countries), Selank (Russia), Semax (Russia), GHRP-2 / pralmorelin (diagnostic use, Japan).

Investigational / sold as research chemicals (US, not approved): CJC-1295, Ipamorelin, GHK-Cu (sold as a cosmetic ingredient), LL-37, Melanotan II, DSIP, Dihexa, Pinealon, AOD-9604, GHRP-6, Hexarelin, IGF-1 LR3, Kisspeptin.

board.category: Coming Down the Pipeline (newest late-stage candidates)
items: Retatrutide (Eli Lilly) — GLP-1/GIP/glucagon triple agonist; Phase 3.
Cagrilintide / CagriSema (Novo Nordisk) — amylin analog ± semaglutide; Phase 3.

board.category: In Human Trials (clinical or early-human data; not yet FDA-approved)
items: Kisspeptin, Selank, Semax, CJC-1295, Melanotan II, GHRP-2, AOD-9604, Thymosin Alpha-1, DSIP, LL-37, Epitalon, Pinealon.
GZR18 (bofanglutide) — long-acting GLP-1 RA (Gan & Lee); Phase 2b vs semaglutide in type 2 diabetes published Jun 2026 (NCT06256549); investigational, not FDA-approved.

board.category: In Animal / Preclinical Studies (little or no human data yet)
items: BPC-157, TB-500 (Thymosin Beta-4), Ipamorelin, GHK-Cu (systemic/injected), MOTS-c, Dihexa, GHRP-6, Hexarelin, IGF-1 LR3, KPV.

timeline.title: This week in peptides: ~8 days out, the four statutory tests the committee applies to each peptide
timeline.content: With the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting about eight days away on July 23-24, 2026, it helps to understand the yardstick the panel actually uses. Under the Section 503A framework, the FDA evaluates each nominated bulk drug substance against four statutory factors - the substance's physical and chemical characterization; any safety issues raised by its use in compounded products; the available evidence of effectiveness (or lack of it); and the substance's historical use in compounding - and then asks the committee whether it should be added to the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List. Per the agency's posted briefing materials, FDA reviewers concluded the seven nominated research peptides fall short on characterization, human effectiveness evidence, and safety, and recommend against adding any of them - BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, and MOTS-c (each in free-base and acetate-salt forms) on July 23, then Emideltide (DSIP), Semax, and Epitalon on July 24. Context to keep straight: the committee's vote is advisory and any change would still require FDA rulemaking; BPC-157 came off the FDA's Category 2 "do-not-compound" list effective April 23, 2026 (removal is not authorization to compound); and the agency's separate proposal to exclude semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide from the 503B bulks list remains in post-comment review. General research only - not medical advice. Source: [FDA 503A bulk substances](https://www.fda.gov/drugs/human-drug-compounding/bulk-drug-substances-used-compounding-under-section-503a-fdc-act), [FDA.gov PCAC meeting](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [FDA briefing document](https://www.fda.gov/media/193343/download)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: ~9 days out, a reminder that the PCAC vote is advisory — the FDA makes the final call
timeline.content: With the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting now about nine days away on July 23-24, 2026, the committee's role is worth keeping in perspective: FDA advisory committees issue non-binding recommendations, and the agency itself makes the final determination on whether a substance is added to the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List. FDA's own posted briefing materials already recommend against adding any of the seven nominated research peptides (BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, MOTS-c on July 23; Emideltide/DSIP, Semax, Epitalon on July 24, each in free-base and acetate-salt forms), citing insufficient characterization and inadequate human efficacy and safety data. Even a favorable vote would only begin FDA rulemaking rather than authorize compounding. BPC-157 remains off the Category 2 do-not-compound list (effective April 23, 2026, which is not authorization to compound), and the separate 503B GLP-1 exclusion proposal is in post-comment review. General research only - not medical advice. Source: [FDA.gov PCAC meeting](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [FDA briefing document](https://www.fda.gov/media/193343/download), [Drug Topics](https://www.drugtopics.com/view/fda-panel-to-evaluate-7-popular-peptides-for-compounding-substances-list)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: with ~11 days to the PCAC vote, the FDA's case centers on injectable immunogenicity and aggregation risk
timeline.content: About 11 days before the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting on July 23-24, 2026, coverage of the agency's posted briefing materials clarifies that its recommendation against adding the seven nominated research peptides (BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, MOTS-c, DSIP/Emideltide, Semax, Epitalon) to the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List rests on a lack of clinical safety data and, for the injectable peptides, immunogenicity concerns tied to potential aggregation and peptide-related impurities. These are advisory briefing proposals, not final determinations, and the committee still votes on July 23-24. General research only - not medical advice. Source: [FDA briefing document](https://www.fda.gov/media/193343/download), [FDA.gov PCAC meeting](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [AJMC](https://www.ajmc.com/view/fda-to-convene-expert-panel-on-expanding-access-to-peptides-amid-safety-regulatory-debate)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: the July 9 comment-relay window has closed — now a ~13-day countdown to the PCAC vote, with each peptide weighed in two chemical forms
timeline.content: The July 9, 2026 cutoff for written comments guaranteed to reach FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) members before deliberation has now passed, leaving roughly 13 days to the July 23-24, 2026 vote; the docket (FDA-2025-N-6895) stays open through July 22 but later comments are not guaranteed to be relayed. The committee will weigh each of the seven nominated research peptides in both free-base and acetate-salt forms - BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, and MOTS-c on July 23, then Emideltide (DSIP), Semax, and Epitalon on July 24 - for the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List, in a session open to the public in person at FDA White Oak and via online teleconference, after briefing materials that recommend against adding any of the seven. General research only - not medical advice. Source: [FDA.gov](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [Federal Register](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/04/16/2026-07361/pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-notice-of-meeting-establishment-of-a-public-docket-request), [Drug Topics](https://www.drugtopics.com/view/fda-panel-to-evaluate-7-popular-peptides-for-compounding-substances-list)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: FDA's PCAC briefing materials are out — and they recommend against adding all seven peptides
timeline.content: Ahead of the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting on July 23–24, 2026, the FDA has posted its scientific briefing documents for the seven nominated research peptides, and across all seven the agency's reviewers recommend they NOT be added to the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List. FDA cites that the substances are not well-characterized, that there is little or no human evidence of effectiveness for the proposed (mostly injectable) routes, and that human safety data are insufficient — including unassessed immunogenicity risk — across BPC-157 (evaluated for ulcerative colitis), KPV, TB-500, and MOTS-c on day one and Emideltide (DSIP), Semax, and Epitalon on day two. These are advisory-committee briefing proposals, not final determinations: the committee still votes on July 23–24 and any change would require subsequent FDA rulemaking. July 9, 2026 is also the cutoff for written comments to docket FDA-2025-N-6895 to be guaranteed relay to committee members. General research only — not medical advice. Source: [FDA.gov PCAC meeting & briefing materials](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [Drug Topics](https://www.drugtopics.com/view/fda-panel-to-evaluate-7-popular-peptides-for-compounding-substances-list)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: holiday-weekend status check — the July 9 comment-relay cutoff is now days away, and FDA's briefing materials come next
timeline.content: Over the Independence Day weekend the regulatory calendar around the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting on July 23–24, 2026 continues to tighten. Per the Federal Register notice, written comments to docket FDA-2025-N-6895 on the seven nominated research peptides — BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, and MOTS-c (day one), then Emideltide (DSIP), Semax, and Epitalon (day two) — are accepted through July 22, 2026, but only those received by July 9, 2026 are guaranteed to reach committee members before they deliberate, putting that cutoff just days out. FDA's advisory-committee practice is to post its scientific briefing materials at least two business days before the meeting, and such documents typically set out the agency's own recommendation — so the next substantive signal on where FDA stands is expected in the days just ahead of July 23. Context to keep straight: BPC-157 was removed from the FDA's Category 2 "do-not-compound" list effective late April 2026 (removal is not authorization to compound), and the agency's separate proposal to exclude semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide from the 503B Bulk Drug Substances List is in its post-comment review phase after that window closed June 29, 2026. General research only — not medical advice. Source: [FDA.gov PCAC meeting](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [Federal Register PCAC notice](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/04/16/2026-07361/pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-notice-of-meeting-establishment-of-a-public-docket-request), [FDA.gov 503B proposal](https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-proposes-exclude-semaglutide-tirzepatide-and-liraglutide-503b-bulks-list)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: even a favorable July 23–24 vote would only start a 12–24 month rulemaking clock
timeline.content: With the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting set for July 23–24, 2026, legal and regulatory analysts are emphasizing what a "yes" vote would — and would not — mean. A positive PCAC recommendation for any of the seven nominated research peptides (BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, and MOTS-c on July 23; Emideltide/DSIP, Semax, and Epitalon on July 24) only initiates FDA rulemaking to add a substance to the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List, a notice-and-comment process that commentators note typically runs roughly 12–24 months before any final rule takes effect. In other words, the hearing shapes the direction of travel but would not, by itself, make these peptides immediately eligible for patient-specific compounding, and "compounding eligibility" remains legally distinct from "FDA approval" of a drug. Separately, BPC-157 already came off the FDA's Category 2 "do-not-compound" list effective April 23, 2026, and the agency's distinct proposal to exclude semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide from the 503B bulks list is now in its post-comment review phase. General research only — not medical advice. Source: [FDA.gov PCAC meeting](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [National Law Review](https://natlawreview.com/article/tiny-chains-big-changes-what-fdas-latest-actions-mean-peptide-compounding), [Lengea Law](https://lengealaw.com/fda-puts-bpc-157-tb-500-and-5-other-peptides-under-the-microscope-what-prescribers-need-to-know-about-the-503a-review/)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: both FDA comment clocks have run out; the July 9 relay cutoff is the last lever before the hearing
timeline.content: With the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting on July 23–24, 2026 now roughly three weeks away, two procedural windows have closed. The deadline to request an oral presentation passed on June 30, 2026, and the public-comment window on the FDA's separate proposal to exclude semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide from the 503B Bulk Drug Substances List closed June 29, 2026 — moving both matters into the agency's review phase. For the seven nominated research peptides slated for the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List — BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, and MOTS-c on day one, then Emideltide (DSIP), Semax, and Epitalon on day two — written comments to docket FDA-2025-N-6895 are still accepted through July 22, 2026, but only those received by July 9, 2026 are guaranteed to reach committee members before they deliberate, making that July 9 cutoff the last practical lever for public input. Context worth keeping straight: BPC-157 came off the FDA's Category 2 "do-not-compound" list effective April 23, 2026, and a positive PCAC recommendation would only begin FDA rulemaking to add a substance to the 503A list — "compounding eligibility" remains distinct from "FDA approval." General research only — not medical advice. Source: [FDA.gov PCAC meeting](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [Federal Register PCAC notice](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/04/16/2026-07361/pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-notice-of-meeting-establishment-of-a-public-docket-request), [FDA.gov 503B proposal](https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-proposes-exclude-semaglutide-tirzepatide-and-liraglutide-503b-bulks-list)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: oral-testimony deadline hits as the GLP-1 bulk-list comment window closes
timeline.content: Two procedural clocks tick over around the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting on July 23–24, 2026. Per the Federal Register notice, today (June 30, 2026) is the deadline to request to make an oral presentation at the meeting, where seven peptides are slated for 503A Bulk Drug Substances List review — BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, and MOTS-c on day one, then Emideltide (DSIP), Semax, and Epitalon on day two. Written comments to docket FDA-2025-N-6895 are accepted through July 22, 2026, but only those received by July 9, 2026 are guaranteed to reach committee members before they deliberate. Separately, the public-comment window on the FDA's distinct proposal to exclude semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide from the 503B Bulk Drug Substances List was set to close June 29, 2026, moving that GLP-1 bulk-compounding question into the agency's review phase. The compounding-eligibility review for research peptides and the bulk-list exclusion for approved GLP-1 drugs remain on parallel but independent tracks. General research only — not medical advice. Source: [Federal Register PCAC notice](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/04/16/2026-07361/pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-notice-of-meeting-establishment-of-a-public-docket-request), [FDA.gov PCAC meeting](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [FDA.gov 503B proposal](https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-proposes-exclude-semaglutide-tirzepatide-and-liraglutide-503b-bulks-list)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: the comment calendar sharpens as two dockets reach key cutoffs
timeline.content: The procedural timeline around the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting on July 23–24, 2026 has come into sharper focus. Per the Federal Register notice, written comments on the seven nominated peptides — BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, and MOTS-c (day one) and Emideltide/DSIP, Semax, and Epitalon (day two) — may be submitted to docket FDA-2025-N-6895 through July 22, 2026, but only comments received by July 9, 2026 are guaranteed to be relayed to committee members before they vote. Separately, the public-comment window on the FDA's distinct proposal to exclude semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide from the 503B Bulk Drug Substances List is set to close June 29, 2026, after which the agency reviews submissions before any final determination. The two proceedings remain on parallel but independent tracks — a compounding-eligibility review for research peptides versus a bulk-list exclusion for approved GLP-1 drugs. General research only — not medical advice. Source: [Federal Register PCAC notice](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/04/16/2026-07361/pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-notice-of-meeting-establishment-of-a-public-docket-request), [FDA.gov PCAC meeting](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [FDA.gov 503B proposal](https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-proposes-exclude-semaglutide-tirzepatide-and-liraglutide-503b-bulks-list)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: two FDA tracks diverge as the July hearing's public comment deadline approaches
timeline.content: Reporting around the upcoming FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee meeting (July 23–24, 2026) highlights a divergence in the agency's posture. On the compounding side, seven peptides are slated for review for the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List — BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, and MOTS-c on July 23, then Emideltide (DSIP), Semax, and Epitalon on July 24 — with the public-comment docket (FDA-2025-N-6895) open through July 9, 2026; this sits alongside the FDA's separate move to tighten 503B bulk compounding of GLP-1 drugs. For contrast, a mitochondria-targeted peptide that pursued the full approval route, elamipretide (Forzinity), holds FDA accelerated approval for Barth syndrome (September 2025) — a reminder that "FDA approval" and "compounding eligibility" are distinct regulatory pathways. General research only — not medical advice. Source: [FDA.gov PCAC meeting](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [Drug Topics](https://www.drugtopics.com/view/fda-set-to-review-peptide-access-for-compounding-pharmacies), [FDA.gov Barth approval](https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-grants-accelerated-approval-first-treatment-barth-syndrome)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: GLP-1 503B comment window closes as the July peptide hearing nears
timeline.content: With roughly four weeks until the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee meeting on July 23–24, 2026, the public comment window on the FDA's separate proposal to exclude semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide from the 503B Bulk Drug Substances List is set to close June 29, 2026. At the July meeting the committee is scheduled to weigh BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, and MOTS-c on day one (July 23) and Emideltide (delta sleep-inducing peptide/DSIP), Semax, and Epitalon on day two (July 24) for possible inclusion on the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List. The FDA's 503B proposal states the agency did not identify sufficient evidence of a clinical need for outsourcing-facility bulk compounding of the three GLP-1 drugs. General research only — not medical advice. Source: [FDA.gov meeting page](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [FDA.gov 503B proposal](https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-proposes-exclude-semaglutide-tirzepatide-and-liraglutide-503b-bulks-list)

timeline.title: This week in peptides: countdown to the FDA's July 23–24 compounding review
timeline.content: Attention is building ahead of the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee meeting on July 23–24, 2026, where BPC-157, TB-500, MOTS-c, KPV, Semax, Epitalon and Emideltide are slated for review for possible inclusion on the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List, and BPC-157 is being moved off the restrictive Category 2 list. Public comment dockets remain open. General research only — not medical advice. Source: [FDA.gov](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [Drug Topics](https://www.drugtopics.com/view/fda-set-to-review-peptide-access-for-compounding-pharmacies)

timeline.title: Comment period closes on FDA proposal to bar bulk compounding of GLP-1s
timeline.content: The public comment window for the FDA's proposal to exclude semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide from the 503B Bulk Drug Substances List closes June 29, 2026. If finalized, the rule would end large-scale compounding of these GLP-1 drugs by outsourcing facilities, though limited patient-specific 503A compounding could continue; legal and industry analysts expect a final rule in late summer 2026. Source: [Orrick](https://www.orrick.com/en/Insights/2026/05/FDA-Moves-to-Shut-the-Door-on-Large-Scale-Compounding-of-GLP1-Drugs), [Pharmacy Times](https://www.pharmacytimes.com/view/fda-moves-to-permanently-close-the-door-on-compounded-glp-1s)

timeline.title: FDA opens public docket ahead of July PCAC peptide review (developing)
timeline.content: The FDA's public comment window for the July 23–24 Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee review of seven peptide bulk substances (docket FDA-2025-N-6895) is open through July 9, 2026; BPC-157 is specifically being evaluated for a potential ulcerative-colitis use on the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List. Source: [FDA.gov](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [Lengea Law](https://lengealaw.com/fda-puts-bpc-157-tb-500-and-5-other-peptides-under-the-microscope-what-prescribers-need-to-know-about-the-503a-review/)

timeline.title: FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee to review 7 peptides
timeline.content: The PCAC is scheduled to evaluate BPC-157, TB-500, MOTS-c, KPV, Semax, Epitalon, and Emideltide for inclusion on the 503A Bulk Drug Substances List; BPC-157 is being moved off the restrictive Category 2 list. Source: [FDA.gov](https://www.fda.gov/advisory-committees/advisory-committee-calendar/july-23-24-2026-meeting-pharmacy-compounding-advisory-committee-07232026), [Drug Topics](https://www.drugtopics.com/view/fda-set-to-review-peptide-access-for-compounding-pharmacies)
